params.text:
What “behaviour that challenges” means

Behaviour that challenges describes actions that are difficult for others to manage, or that put the person or those around them at risk of harm — shouting, hitting, throwing things, refusing care, or withdrawing completely. The phrase describes the behaviour, not the person. It is never a label for a “difficult” or “aggressive” individual. Understood correctly, behaviour that challenges is almost always a sign of distress and an attempt to communicate a need that has not been met.

  • Describes the behaviour: the term names an action and its impact, not a person’s character.
  • A sign of distress: the behaviour usually signals that something is wrong for the person.
  • Common in care: many people you support may show it at some point, especially at times of change or discomfort.

params.alt: Illustration for What the term means

params.text:
Why we avoid labelling people

Labelling someone as “aggressive”, “attention-seeking” or “a challenge” changes how a whole team sees and treats them, and it stops us asking the most important question: what is this person trying to tell us? When we blame the person, we look for ways to control them; when we see distress, we look for the unmet need behind it and respond with compassion.

This course raises your awareness of behaviour that challenges and how to respond calmly, safely and with respect. It is knowledge-level learning only. Physical intervention is a last resort that requires specific, accredited training, and nothing here teaches or authorises you to physically restrain anyone. This course does not replace supervised, hands-on training, and you must always work within your employer’s policies, the person’s care plan, and the limits of your own training and competence.
